title: Personal Information
shortSummary: Share your name, country, age, and what you do, the basics of any introduction in Hindi.
culturalNote: When Indians meet, early questions about your hometown (आप कहाँ से हैं?), your work, and your family are signs of friendliness, not nosiness; they help place you in a social web. People often ask 'what do you do?' early because profession carries a lot of social weight. Many Indians are multilingual, so it is common to ask which languages you speak (आप कौन सी भाषाएँ बोलते हैं?), and switching between Hindi and English mid-sentence (Hinglish) is completely normal. Age and marital status may come up sooner than Western visitors expect, especially from older relatives, and a vague answer is perfectly acceptable. Using ji (जी) after a name or as a standalone 'yes' adds politeness, as in Sharma-ji.

grammar.rule: The verb 'to be' (hona) in the present
grammar.explanation: The verb hona (होना, to be) changes with the subject: main hoon (I am), tum ho (you informal are), aap/ve hain (you polite/they are), vah hai (he/she is). It always sits at the end of the sentence.
grammar.commonMistake: Using hai (singular) with main; the correct form is main hoon.

grammar.rule: Verb agreement with the speaker's gender
grammar.explanation: Hindi verbs in many tenses agree with the gender of the subject. A man says rehta hoon (I live) and karta hoon (I do); a woman says rehti hoon and karti hoon. The ending switches -ta to -ti for feminine subjects.

examples.native: मैं दिल्ली में रहता हूँ। (main Dilli mein rehta hoon.)
मैं दिल्ली में रहती हूँ। (main Dilli mein rehti hoon.)

grammar.commonMistake: A woman using the -ta ending; she should use -ti to match her gender.

dialogue.setup: Anjali, an Indian student, chats with Tom, an Australian traveller, at a language meetup.

lines.speaker: Anjali
lines.native: नमस्ते! मेरा नाम अंजली है। आपका नाम क्या है?
lines.transliteration: namaste! mera naam Anjali hai. aapka naam kya hai?
lines.english: Hello! My name is Anjali. What is your name?

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.native: नमस्ते अंजली! मैं टॉम हूँ। मैं ऑस्ट्रेलिया से हूँ।
lines.transliteration: namaste Anjali! main Tom hoon. main Australia se hoon.
lines.english: Hello Anjali! I'm Tom. I'm from Australia.

lines.speaker: Anjali
lines.native: अच्छा! आप क्या काम करते हैं?
lines.transliteration: achha! aap kya kaam karte hain?
lines.english: Oh nice! What work do you do?

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.native: मैं एक इंजीनियर हूँ। और मैं हिंदी सीख रहा हूँ।
lines.transliteration: main ek engineer hoon. aur main Hindi seekh raha hoon.
lines.english: I'm an engineer. And I'm learning Hindi.

lines.speaker: Anjali
lines.native: बहुत अच्छा! आप कहाँ रहते हैं?
lines.transliteration: bahut achha! aap kahaan rehte hain?
lines.english: Very good! Where do you live?

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.native: मैं सिडनी में रहता हूँ। आप कहाँ से हैं?
lines.transliteration: main Sydney mein rehta hoon. aap kahaan se hain?
lines.english: I live in Sydney. Where are you from?

lines.speaker: Anjali
lines.native: मैं जयपुर से हूँ। आपसे मिलकर खुशी हुई!
lines.transliteration: main Jaipur se hoon. aapse milkar khushi hui!
lines.english: I'm from Jaipur. Nice to meet you!

quiz.question: How do you say 'Where are you from?' (polite) in Hindi?
quiz.correctAnswer: आप कहाँ से हैं? (aap kahaan se hain?)
quiz.explanation: Kahaan means 'where' and se means 'from'; the polite aap takes hain.

quiz.question: A woman saying 'I live in Delhi' should say:
quiz.explanation: Rehti is the feminine form; rehta is for a man.

quiz.question: मैं भारत _____ हूँ। (I am from India)
Which correctly completes 'मैं छात्र ___' (I am a student)?
quiz.explanation: With main (I), the verb hona becomes hoon.

quiz.question: Arrange: है / नाम / मेरा / टॉम (My name is Tom)
quiz.correctAnswer: मेरा नाम टॉम है (mera naam Tom hai)
quiz.explanation: Possessive first, then noun, then name, then the verb hai at the end.
